sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, LEFT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 6, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 5 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see what's happening! The nodes swapped positions:
- Node 33 was the white divider block at [[38,32],[39,33]], and it moved to [[38,10],[39,11]] - the position where the green pixel originally was!
- Node 34 was the green pixel at [[40,10],[41,11]], and it moved to [[38,32],[39,33]] - the divider block's position!
So UP/DOWN swaps the active object with the divider block at the same row level. The green pixel (node 34) swapped with the white divider block (node 33).
This means:
- UP/DOWN moves objects to/from the divider (swapping with divider blocks)
- LEFT/RIGHT moves objects within their container
The game mechanic seems to be: select an object, move it to the divider (swap with a divider block), which transfers it to the other side.
Now I need to figure out:
1. How to select different objects (maybe MOUSE clicks select them)
2. What the goal configuration is
Looking at the right side, there are two white containers:
- One with red inside (top)
- One with blue inside (bottom)
On the left side, there are:
- A gray container with yellow inside (top area)
- A blue square
- Red and dark red blocks
- A pattern of blue/charcoal pixels
- A gray container with light green inside (bottom area)
The goal might be to move objects from left to right to fill the white containers, or to match colors.
But first, I need to undo the swap I just did. Let me try DOWN to swap the green pixel back.
Wait, but the node IDs swapped too - node 33 is now green and node 34 is now white. The colors are tied to the nodes, not the positions. So the green pixel is now at the divider and the white block is where the green pixel was.
Let me try DOWN to see if it swaps back.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The green pixel (node 34) swapped with white divider block (node 33)!
# Node 33 (was white divider) is now at [[38,10],[39,11]] with color N (green!)
# Node 34 (was green pixel) is now at [[38,32],[39,33]] with color W (white!)
# So UP swaps the active object with the divider block at that row

# Try DOWN to swap back
action(['DOWN'])
print(f"After DOWN: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = {n['id']: n for n in seg['nodes']}

# Check green and white nodes
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [33, 34]:
        print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Also check if the green pixel is back in its container
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green object: id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves active object right 2px within container", "LEFT": "Moves active object left 2px within container", "UP": "Swaps active object with divider block at same row level", "DOWN": "Likely swaps active object with divider block below", "MOUSE": "Selects objects"}, "object_taxonomy": {"divider_blocks": "White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, can be swapped with objects", "colored_pixels": "Small movable objects in containers"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White bar at row 63, shrinks as timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "UP swaps active object with divider block. Green pixel (node 34) swapped with white divider (node 33). The swap exchanges both position AND color - nodes keep their identity but swap locations.", "goal_model": "Move colored objects from left containers through divider to right side to match targets in white containers", "recent_findings": ["UP/DOWN swaps objects with divider blocks", "Node 33 (white) moved to green pixel position, node 34 (green) moved to divider position"], "open_questions": ["How to select different objects?", "What is the target configuration?"], "current_plan": "Try DOWN to reverse the swap, then figure out selection", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
